mnemonics: D.C. Comics Shrinks Villains: Decrease (problem size) and Conquer (sub-problem) like a superhero breaking down a big villain into smaller, manageable minions.
Think 'Divide' but without the 'Conquer' part first - it's 'Decrease' (make it smaller) THEN 'Conquer' (solve the smaller version).
keyPoints: Solves a problem by exploiting a relationship between a solution for a given instance and a solution for a smaller instance of the same problem.
Often implemented using iteration or recursion, frequently with a time complexity of O(n).
Common variations include Decrease-by-a-Constant (e.g., by 1: insertion sort) and Decrease-by-a-Constant-Factor (e.g., by 2: binary search).
commonMistakes: Confusing it with Divide-and-Conquer. Decrease-and-Conquer works on a single smaller sub-problem, while Divide-and-Conquer splits the problem into multiple (usually two) sub-problems.
Incorrectly assuming recursion is always necessary; many decrease-and-conquer algorithms (like insertion sort) are efficiently implemented iteratively.
